ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Kueh Bangkit. No sufficiently specific parent term for Peranakan tapioca-coconut festive cookies found in FoodOn as of 2026-04-16.
ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — closest parent used; no FoodEx2 code specific to Peranakan tapioca-coconut cookies exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kueh Bangkit is 5 small cookies, weighing approximately 40g, providing around 180 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Kueh Bangkit — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kueh Bangkit. HPB Foodo is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tapioca/sago starch-based foods — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on tapioca starch GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kueh Bangkit exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~75 is estimated from tapioca/sago starch-based foods, which are near-pure amylopectin starch with GI values of approximately 70–85 in peer-reviewed literature. GL per 40g serving = (75 × 12g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.0. Available carbohydrate per 40g serving = 40 × 0.30 = 12g. This is a defensible analogous estimate consistent with the amylopectin-dominant starch profile of tapioca flour.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~20mg per 40g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Kueh Bangkit contains approximately 20mg sodium per 5-piece serving, posing no meaningful sodium burden for hypertensive individuals; however, the high saturated fat content (5g per serving) warrants moderation given long-term cardiovascular risk associated with saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~75) from amylopectin-dominant tapioca starch, moderate glycemic load per serving (GL ~9)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kueh Bangkit has a high GI (~75) due to near-pure amylopectin tapioca starch, producing a rapid postprandial glucose spike; the GL per 5-piece serving is moderate (~9), but festive consumption of 10 or more pieces raises GL to ~18 (high), and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit portion size and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (12.5g per 100g; ~5g per 40g serving) from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content derived from coconut milk (12.5g per 100g, approximately 5g per 40g serving) may adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels with regular or high-volume consumption, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Bangkit (also spelled Kuih Bangkit) are traditional Peranakan festive cookies originating from Singapore and Malaysia, made primarily from tapioca (sago) flour, coconut milk, egg yolk, and sugar. The cookies are characteristically small, white, crumbly, and melt-in-the-mouth in texture, shaped using decorative wooden moulds and gently baked. The name 'Bangkit' means 'to rise' or 'to lift' in Malay, referring to the action of pressing and lifting the dough from the mould. They are a staple festive treat served during Chinese New Year and Hari Raya Aidilfitri. Per 5-piece serving (~40g): approximately 180 kcal, 1g protein, 6g fat (~5g saturated), 12g carbohydrate, 12g sugar, GI ~75 (high), GL ~9.0, ~20mg sodium, and zero dietary fibre. Per 100g: 450 kcal, 2.5g protein, 15g fat, 12.5g saturated fat, 30g carbohydrate, 30g sugar, 50mg sodium. The very high GI is attributable to the amylopectin-dominant structure of tapioca starch. Tapioca flour contains negligible dietary fibre. The saturated fat derives entirely from coconut milk. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (no animal fat used). Nutritionally, Kueh Bangkit is a high-energy, high-GI, high-saturated-fat festive snack with minimal micronutrient value. Clinically, it is suitable for occasional consumption in small portions for most individuals, but requires strict portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes due to the rapid glucose spike from tapioca starch, and moderation for those with cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat load. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data on tapioca/sago starch-based foods, which is the closest defensible approved GI source. All core macros are complete and internally consistent. Confidence is high.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: tapioca flour is a refined starch with negligible dietary fibre content, confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo and consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for tapioca starch (USDA SR Legacy). cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero: no animal fat is used in the standard Kueh Bangkit formulation; egg yolk contributes minimal cholesterol at typical recipe proportions and HPB Foodo reports 0mg. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kueh Bangkit not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Kueh Bangkit not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16. GI ~75 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca/sago starch-based foods from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement for Kueh Bangkit exists. GL per 40g serving calculated as (75 × 12g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.0. FoodEx2 code A0GBQ used as closest parent (biscuits/cookies); no exact FoodEx2 code for Kueh Bangkit exists. No FoodOn URI or SNOMED CT code found for this food.
